book_claims.claim: 53% reduced risk of Crohn's disease with higher consumption of vegetables and fruit
book_claims.mechanism: Pectin and other fruit fibres feed beneficial gut bacteria and produce anti-inflammatory short-chain fatty acids

book_claims.claim: Provide antioxidant vitamins (especially vitamin C) that protect against oxidative damage
book_claims.mechanism: Vitamin C neutralises free radicals, prevents LDL oxidation

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis (Aune et al., 2017) of 95 prospective studies found that 800g/day of fruits and vegetables was associated with 31% lower risk of premature death, with significant benefits starting at just 200g/day.
Prospective study (Wang et al., PLOS Medicine 2021) of 100,000+ participants found 2 servings/day of fruit associated with 12% lower risk of cardiovascular disease and 10% lower all-cause mortality.

Systematic review (Slavin & Lloyd, 2012; updated meta-analyses) confirmed dietary fibre from fruit reduces risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and colorectal cancer, with polyphenols contributing independent anti-inflammatory effects.

culinary_pairings.source: Traditional dessert pairing: dried fruits with nuts
Traditional sweetener complement for seasonal fruits
Fruit + whole grain fibre for sustained energy

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Citrus fruits, berries, and kiwi are richest sources. Heat and light degrade vitamin C; eat fresh when possible.
Pectin is a soluble fibre fermented by gut bacteria to produce butyrate and propionate, key short-chain fatty acids.
Berries and dark-skinned fruits highest; gut microbiota metabolism enhances bioactivity.

synergies.description: Traditional pairing as dessert: dried fruits with nuts
Combined fruit and vegetable intake provides diverse phytochemicals and antioxidants
